title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
This is the foundation document for the Competencies category. It explains what competency-based (also called behavioural or structured) interviewing is, summarises the published evidence for why employers across the UK and EU use it, and organises the competencies that recur across major frameworks into eight recurring **competency families**: working with others; communication and influencing; leadership and management; problem-solving and analysis; delivering results; resilience and adaptability; integrity and values; and customer and stakeholder focus. It then maps those families onto three named public frameworks — the UK Civil Service Success Profiles Behaviours [2], the EPSO general competencies used for EU-institution selection [4], and the NHS values [6] — and onto the competency labels most commonly assessed in private-sector and graduate-scheme recruitment [15].

The document serves both audiences of this knowledge base. Candidates learn how to recognise which competency a question is really testing from its wording, so preparation effort lands where it counts. Interviewers and HR practitioners see how the major frameworks define and group competencies, and why professional-body and academic evidence favours structured, criterion-scored interviewing over unstructured conversation [12] [18].

Document-level confidence is **Medium**. The framework definitions, competency lists and validity evidence rest on official and academic sources and would individually merit High confidence; however, the eight-family taxonomy and the cross-framework mapping table are this knowledge base's own reasoned synthesis (clearly labelled as such), and a document whose central organising device is synthesis is rated Medium under the confidence rubric.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
- Competency-based interviews ask candidates for real past examples ("Tell me about a time when…") and score the answers against pre-agreed criteria; the CIPD describes structured interviews as asking a predefined set of questions, in the same order, to all candidates, with responses scored against consistent criteria. *(documented)* [12]
- The evidence case is strong: in the most recent large re-analysis of selection-method validity, structured interviews emerged as the top-ranked selection procedure for predicting job performance. *(documented)* [18] [19]
- The UK Civil Service assesses nine named Behaviours (e.g. Working Together, Communicating and Influencing, Delivering at Pace) as part of Success Profiles [1] [2]; EPSO assesses eight general competencies (e.g. Working Together, Communication, Decision-Making and Getting Results) defined in its 2023 Competency Framework [3] [4]. *(documented)*
- The same underlying families recur across public-sector, EU and graduate-scheme frameworks under different names — a candidate who prepares one strong example per family can adapt it across employers. *(guidance)*
- Question wording signals the competency being tested: stems such as "worked in a team", "persuade/explain", "led", "difficult decision", "tight deadline", "setback/pressure" each point to a different family. *(guidance, illustrated with sourced questions below)*
- Interviewers should ask each applicant the same questions where possible and base decisions on the job description and person specification — this is ACAS guidance and also reduces discrimination risk under the Equality Act 2010. *(documented)* [7] [11]
- Graduate-scheme competition is intense — ISE data reported an average of 140 applications per graduate job in 2024 — which is one reason large employers rely on structured, criterion-scored assessment at scale. *(documented)* [17]
- The eight-family taxonomy used in this knowledge base is an editorial organising device, not an official standard; always check the named framework an employer actually uses. *(guidance)*
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text: This document answers two questions. For candidates: *"What competencies do UK and EU employers actually assess in interviews, and how do I tell which one a question is testing?"* For interviewers and HR: *"What are the recognised competency families, how do the major frameworks define them, and what does good evidence-based practice look like when mapping questions to competencies?"* It is the parent document for the per-competency deep-dives in this category, which take each family to depth; this document deliberately stays broad.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Covered:** the definition and evidence base of competency-based/structured interviewing; eight recurring competency families; mapping of those families onto the UK Civil Service Success Profiles Behaviours, the EPSO general competencies, NHS values (briefly), and common private-sector/graduate-scheme competency labels; how question wording signals the competency assessed; a starter question bank spanning the families; and the legal and fairness essentials for UK and EU markets.

**Not covered (deliberate exclusions):** per-competency depth — level descriptors, grade-by-grade expectations and extended model answers belong to the child documents (teamwork-collaboration, communication-influencing, leadership-management, problem-solving-decision-making, delivering-results-planning, resilience-adaptability, integrity-values-ethics, customer-stakeholder-service, all planned); detailed treatment of interview techniques such as STAR/CARL (see techniques-foundation); full framework guides (see uk-frameworks-foundation and eu-frameworks-foundation); and interview formats such as panels, video and assessment centres (see formats-foundation). Sector-specific frameworks (e.g. policing, teaching) are out of scope here.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
- **Competency / behaviour** — in the Civil Service Success Profiles framework, Behaviours are "the actions and activities that people do which result in effective performance in a job" [2]. "Competency" is the more common generic term for the same idea in private-sector and EU usage [3] [15].
- **Competency-based (behavioural) interview** — an interview in which candidates draw on examples from their own experience to demonstrate particular competencies; targetjobs describes it as one where "recruiters will ask questions where you will need to draw on examples from your life so far to demonstrate times when you have employed particular competencies" [15].
- **Structured interview** — per CIPD guidance, an interview using a predefined set of questions, asked in the same order to all candidates, with responses scored using consistent criteria against pre-agreed criteria for each question [12].
- **Anchor** — in the EPSO Competency Framework, an observable element of a general competency used to recognise and assess it [4].
- **Competency family** — this knowledge base's term for a cluster of closely related competencies that recur across frameworks under different names. An editorial grouping, not an official framework term.
- **STAR** — Situation, Task, Action, Result: a widely recommended structure for competency answers [13]. Treated fully in techniques-foundation.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## UK Civil Service: Success Profiles Behaviours

Success Profiles are the recruitment framework used within the UK Civil Service. A Success Profile can draw on five elements — Behaviours, Strengths, Ability, Experience and Technical — with the mix depending on the role [1]. The Behaviours element defines nine named behaviours [2]:

1. Seeing the Big Picture
2. Changing and Improving
3. Making Effective Decisions
4. Leadership
5. Communicating and Influencing
6. Working Together
7. Developing Self and Others
8. Managing a Quality Service
9. Delivering at Pace

Behaviours are assessed in several ways across the recruitment process, including written examples at application, interviews, and job-related simulations; candidates may be asked to give examples of when they have demonstrated a particular behaviour [2].

## EU institutions: EPSO general competencies

The European Personnel Selection Office (EPSO) selects staff for the EU institutions against a Competency Framework that "defines the competencies that are considered essential to perform effectively in any given job" [3]. The framework in use since 2023 defines eight general competencies, each broken into observable anchors [4]:

1. Critical thinking, analysing & creative problem-solving
2. Decision-making & getting results
3. Information management (digital and data literacy)
4. Self-management
5. Working together
6. Learning as a skill
7. Communication
8. Intrapreneurship

For any given competition, the Notice of Competition published in the Official Journal is the only official document containing detailed information about the actual tests used [5].

## NHS (brief)

NHS recruitment in England sits within the values of the NHS Constitution: working together for patients; respect and dignity; commitment to quality of care; compassion; improving lives; and everyone counts [6]. Values-based recruitment against these is treated in the planned NHS framework documents; they are included here because they show the same families appearing as *values* rather than *competencies*.

## Private sector and graduate schemes

There is no single private-sector framework, but the competency labels used in graduate and professional recruitment are highly convergent: targetjobs lists communication, teamwork, problem-solving, leadership, time management, planning and organisation, customer-facing skills, influencing, decision making, motivation, adaptability, commercial awareness and creativity among the competencies graduate employers commonly assess [15]. The scale of graduate recruitment pushes employers towards structured methods: Institute of Student Employers survey data reported by Prospects Luminate found the average employer received 140 applications per graduate job in 2024, a 59% increase on the prior year, across a survey of 145 employers [17].
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## Why employers use competency-based, structured interviews

The professional-body position is clear: CIPD guidance states that structured interviews are better at predicting job performance than unstructured interviews, and that situational and past-behavioural questions are the best kinds of interview questions; consistent questions and objective scoring criteria make responses comparable and reduce the influence of personal bias [12].

The academic evidence supports this. Sackett, Zhang, Berry and Lievens (2022) re-analysed the meta-analytic validity estimates for personnel-selection methods, correcting a systematic overcorrection for range restriction; in their revised estimates, structured interviews emerged as the top-ranked selection procedure, even though absolute validity values for most methods came down from earlier estimates [18]. Their 2023 follow-up draws out the applied implications for selection-system design, including the trade-off between validity and diversity when combining predictors [19].

ACAS guidance to employers reinforces the fairness rationale: prepare questions in advance, ask each applicant the same questions where possible to ensure applicants are treated the same way, use more than one interviewer to reduce the risk of discriminating, and base decisions on how each applicant met each point in the job description and person specification [11].

## The eight competency families and how frameworks name them

> **Labelling note (synthesis):** the eight families below are this knowledge base's editorial grouping of competencies that recur across the cited frameworks. The framework entries in each row are documented [2] [4] [6] [15]; the *grouping* of them into a family is reasoned synthesis, and cells marked "—" mean the framework has no close named equivalent.

| Competency family | Civil Service Behaviours [2] | EPSO general competencies [4] | NHS values [6] | Common graduate-scheme labels [15] |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Working with others | Working Together | Working together | Working together for patients | Teamwork |
| Communication & influencing | Communicating and Influencing | Communication | — | Communication; influencing |
| Leadership & management | Leadership; Developing Self and Others | Intrapreneurship | — | Leadership; motivation; delegation |
| Problem-solving & analysis | Making Effective Decisions; Seeing the Big Picture | Critical thinking, analysing & creative problem-solving | — | Problem-solving; decision making; creativity; commercial awareness |
| Delivering results | Delivering at Pace; Managing a Quality Service | Decision-making & getting results | Commitment to quality of care | Time management; planning and organisation |
| Resilience & adaptability | Changing and Improving | Self-management; Learning as a skill | — | Adaptability |
| Integrity & values | — (values sit in the Civil Service Code, outside the Behaviours list) | — (partially visible in anchors, e.g. treating others "equally and with fairness") | Respect and dignity; compassion; everyone counts | Motivation; values fit |
| Customer & stakeholder focus | Managing a Quality Service | — | Working together for patients; improving lives | Customer-facing skills |

Documented definitions anchoring the table:

- Civil Service **Working Together**: "Form effective partnerships and relationships with people both internally and externally, from a range of diverse backgrounds, sharing information, resources and support" [2].
- Civil Service **Communicating and Influencing**: "Communicate purpose and direction with clarity, integrity and enthusiasm. Respect the needs, responses and opinions of others" [2].
- EPSO **Working together**: "Demonstrates cross-functional cooperation and fosters diversity and inclusion. Cooperates well by seeking compromise, sharing knowledge and assisting others" [4].
- EPSO **Communication**: "Conveys information and opinions clearly, generates buy-in by facilitating interaction and engaging effectively with others" [4].
- EPSO **Critical thinking, analysing & creative problem-solving**: "Thinks holistically and translates complex information into creative and innovative solutions" [4].
- EPSO **Decision-making and getting results**: "Takes decisions in a shifting environment, translates them into action and works towards tangible results" [4].
- EPSO **Self-management**: "Organises own tasks, demonstrates responsibility and a constructive attitude at all times", with anchors including responding calmly to obstacles and adapting to changing work conditions [4].
- EPSO **Intrapreneurship**: "Drives change, inspires and empowers others in implementing change and offers guidance at all levels" [4].

## How question wording signals the competency

Competency questions characteristically open with stems that invite a specific past example — targetjobs and Skills Development Scotland both present competency interviewing as asking how you have used specific skills in past situations [15] [16], and the National Careers Service presents the STAR structure against exactly such prompts (e.g. "Tell me about a time when you have shown leadership skills.") [13]. Published question sets pair recognisable wording cues with the competency assessed: for example, My World of Work maps "Describe a time you collaborated successfully with a team." to teamwork, "Name a time you had to make a challenging decision in the workplace." to decisiveness, and "Can you tell us about a time you had to work under pressure?" to resilience [16]; targetjobs maps "Can you give me an example of when you worked in a team?" to teamwork and "Tell me about a time when you took on a leadership role" to leadership [15]. The signal-reading skill this implies — keyword in the stem → family → prepared example — is developed in the coaching sections below.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
A starter bank spanning the eight families. Provenance per entry: *(sourced …)* questions are quoted from the named published source; *(composed)* questions were written for this knowledge base in the style of the cited frameworks and are not attributed to any employer.

## Working with others

1. Tell me about a time you have worked as part of a team? *(sourced: National Careers Service, The STAR method)* [13]
2. Describe a time you collaborated successfully with a team. *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [16]
3. Describe a situation where you had to build a working relationship with a colleague whose approach was very different from yours. *(composed)*

## Communication & influencing

1. Have you ever had to explain a topic to somebody with limited understanding? *(sourced: targetjobs)* [15]
2. Describe a time you had to persuade a sceptical colleague or stakeholder to accept your recommendation. *(composed)*

## Leadership & management

1. Tell me about a time when you have shown leadership skills. *(sourced: National Careers Service, The STAR method)* [13]
2. Tell me about a time when you took on a leadership role. *(sourced: targetjobs)* [15]
3. Could you tell us about a time you led a complex project? How did you handle it? *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [16]

## Problem-solving & analysis

1. Give me an example of when you faced a problem at work. How did you handle it? *(sourced: National Careers Service, The STAR method)* [13]
2. Name a time you had to make a challenging decision in the workplace. *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [16]
3. Walk me through a time you had to reach a decision with incomplete information. What did you weigh up? *(composed)*

## Delivering results

1. Give an example of a time when you showed initiative. *(sourced: targetjobs)* [15]
2. Tell me about a time you delivered a piece of work to a tight deadline while other priorities competed for your attention. *(composed)*

## Resilience & adaptability

1. Can you tell us about a time you had to work under pressure? *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [16]
2. Give an example of a time when you handled a major crisis. *(sourced: targetjobs)* [15]
3. Tell me about a time a project changed direction late on. How did you adapt your plans? *(composed)*

## Integrity & values

1. Tell me about a time you were asked to do something you believed was wrong. What did you do? *(composed)*
2. Describe a situation where you saw behaviour that fell short of your organisation's values. How did you respond? *(composed)*

## Customer & stakeholder focus

1. Describe a time you had to handle a customer's difficult request. *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [16]
2. Describe a time you improved a service or product based on feedback from a customer or service user. *(composed)*
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.**

- **Structure every example with STAR.** The National Careers Service defines the structure: the Situation you had to deal with, the Task you were given, the Action you took, and the Result — what happened and what you learned [13]. Keep Situation and Task brief; the scored substance is almost always in Action and Result.
- **Prepare one strong, recent example per family, not per question.** Because the same eight families recur across frameworks (see the mapping table above), a bank of six to eight well-rehearsed examples can be adapted to most competency interviews. Choose examples where *you* acted — "I" not "we" — since assessors score your behaviour, not your team's.
- **Match the example's altitude to the role.** A team-leading example serves the leadership family; a peer-persuasion example serves communication and influencing. Read the framework the employer names in the advert (Civil Service adverts name the Behaviours assessed [2]; EPSO notices specify the competencies tested [5]) and select examples that reach the level described.
- **Signal-read the question before answering.** Identify the keyword in the stem (team / explain / led / decision / deadline / pressure / customer), place it in a family, and retrieve the matching prepared example. If a question could fit two families (e.g. "led a project under pressure"), let the interviewer's framing decide which to foreground.
- **Worked skeleton (communication & influencing, composed question 2 above):** Situation — a stakeholder resisted a process change; Task — you needed their sign-off by month-end; Action — you sought their objections first, quantified the benefit in their terms, and offered a pilot; Result — sign-off achieved, pilot adopted. This mirrors the documented definition of Communicating and Influencing — clarity of purpose while respecting "the needs, responses and opinions of others" [2] — and the EPSO Communication anchors on tailoring the message and using convincing arguments [4].
- **End on the Result and, where natural, the learning.** The NCS formulation explicitly includes "what you learned" in the R [13]; a sentence of reflection also serves frameworks that assess learning (EPSO's Learning as a skill [4]).
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

- **What is scored.** In the Civil Service, Behaviours are assessed through written examples, tests, interviews and job simulations; candidates may be asked to give examples of when they have demonstrated a particular behaviour [2]. In EPSO competitions, competencies from the Competency Framework are assessed through the tests specified in the Notice of Competition, which is the only official document detailing the tests for a given competition [5].
- **How scoring is structured.** CIPD describes the structured method: a predefined set of questions asked in the same order to all candidates, with responses scored using consistent criteria against pre-agreed criteria for each question; CIPD also reports evidence that scoring rubrics improve fairness of outcomes [12].
- **Consistency and panel composition.** ACAS advises employers to prepare questions in advance, ask each applicant the same questions where possible so applicants are treated in the same way, and use more than one interviewer to reduce the risk of discriminating; selection decisions should be based on how each applicant met each point in the job description and person specification [11].
- **Observable anchors.** The EPSO framework shows what "evidence" means to an assessor: each competency is decomposed into observable anchors — e.g. for Working together, "Discusses differences of opinion openly with the individuals concerned … and strives for compromise in situations of conflict" [4]. Assessors listen for behaviour matching the anchors, not for opinions or hypotheticals.
- **Why the method is trusted.** The evidence ranking matters to assessors as well as candidates: structured interviews emerged as the top-ranked selection procedure in the revised meta-analytic estimates [18], and the applied literature discusses combining them with other predictors in system design [19].
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.**

- **Answering the wrong family.** Giving a teamwork story to a leadership question wastes the scoring opportunity: assessors score against the criteria for the competency asked (see the structured-scoring practice documented by CIPD [12]). Signal-read first.
- **"We" answers.** Describing the team's achievement without isolating your own actions gives the assessor no behaviour to score against anchors of the kind EPSO publishes [4].
- **Hypotheticals instead of examples.** Competency questions ask for what you *did*; frameworks explicitly ask candidates for examples of when they demonstrated the behaviour [2]. "I would…" answers fail that test unless the interview is deliberately situational.
- **One mega-example for everything.** Reusing a single story across all questions undercuts breadth; the family taxonomy exists precisely because employers sample across distinct behaviours [2] [4].
- **Result-free stories.** Stopping at Action drops the R that the STAR structure and assessors expect [13].
- **For interviewers: improvising questions per candidate.** Varying questions between candidates undermines both comparability and legal defensibility — ACAS advises the same questions where possible and warns that questions must not discriminate [11].
- **For interviewers: labelling a question with one competency and scoring another.** If the advert names the Behaviour assessed [2], score that Behaviour; drifting criteria reintroduces the unstructured-interview weaknesses documented by CIPD [12].
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Coaching layer.**

1. **Build the example bank (60–90 minutes).** Draw the eight-family table and write one candidate example per row from your last two to three years. Mark rows where your example is weak — those are your preparation priorities.
2. **Signal-reading drill (15 minutes).** Take the 19 questions in the Question Bank above in random order and, before answering, name the family each belongs to. Check yourself against the sub-headings.
3. **Timed STAR practice (3 × 10 minutes).** Answer one sourced question aloud in under three minutes using STAR [13]. Record yourself; check that Action and Result took at least two-thirds of the time.
4. **Anchor self-scoring (30 minutes).** Take your Working-with-others example and score it against the published EPSO Working together anchors [4]: did you show compromise-seeking, knowledge-sharing, assisting others? Rewrite the Action section to evidence at least two anchors.
5. **Interviewer drill (pairs, 30 minutes).** Draft three questions for one family, agree scoring criteria *before* the mock interview (per CIPD structured practice [12]), interview your partner, then compare the scores you each expected. Disagreements reveal vague criteria.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited. Signposting only — not legal advice.**

- **UK — Equality Act 2010.** Part 5 of the Act covers work, including recruitment [7]. Section 4 lists nine protected characteristics: age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sex and sexual orientation [8]. ACAS guidance operationalises this for interviews: questions must not discriminate, and employers must ask anyone coming to an interview to say if they need reasonable adjustments to attend — for example wheelchair access, extra time for tests, or a sign-language interpreter [11].
- **EU — equal treatment in selection.** Council Directive 2000/78/EC establishes a general framework for equal treatment in employment and occupation, expressly covering "conditions for access to employment … including selection criteria and recruitment conditions", and prohibits direct and indirect discrimination on grounds of religion or belief, disability, age or sexual orientation, with reasonable-accommodation obligations for disabled persons [9].
- **Candidate data.** Interview notes, scores and application records about identifiable candidates are personal data; in the EU their processing falls under Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR) [10]. Interviewers should assume candidates may exercise data-subject rights over interview records.
- **Fairness by design.** The structured method itself is a fairness control: consistent questions and criterion-based scoring make responses comparable and reduce the scope for bias, per CIPD [12] and ACAS [11].
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
- **The family taxonomy is editorial.** The eight families are a synthesis for navigation and preparation; employers assess against their *own* named framework, and mapping cells marked "—" show real gaps. Always defer to the employer's published framework.
- **Frameworks change.** The EPSO framework in this document dates from 2023 [4], and EPSO has been through an organisational transformation; the Success Profiles guidance is periodically updated on GOV.UK [1]. Verify against the live pages before high-stakes use; review is due 2027-07-19.
- **Validity estimates are contested in magnitude.** Sackett et al. revised headline validity numbers downwards relative to older meta-analyses [18] [19]; older sources quoting higher coefficients are still widely circulated. The *ranking* of structured interviews is robust in the cited work; specific coefficients should be quoted from [18] only.
- **Careers-site question lists are practitioner sources.** The sourced questions from targetjobs and My World of Work [15] [16] evidence *what is published as typical*, not what any specific employer asks.
- **ISE figures are reported second-hand.** The 140-applications-per-job figure is ISE survey data reported via Prospects Luminate by an ISE author [17]; the underlying ISE report is member-gated and was not independently inspected.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
- **Private-sector frameworks by name.** This document evidences graduate-scheme competency labels via careers sources [15]; obtaining named, publicly documented corporate frameworks (e.g. published bank or consultancy competency dictionaries) would strengthen the mapping table. Candidate for the per-competency child documents.
- **NHS values-based recruitment mechanics.** How NHS values [6] translate into interview questions and scoring is only signposted here; the planned NHS/UK-frameworks documents should cover values-based recruitment guidance from NHS Employers.
- **Strengths vs competency mix in graduate selection.** ISE members-only data likely quantifies the strengths-based shift in graduate recruitment; a public, citable figure has not yet been found.
- **Civil Service assessor scoring scales — resolved 2026-07-20.** A central scale is published: Cabinet Office guidance states that "Experience, technical skills and behaviours should be scored using the 7 point scale. Strengths should be scored using the 4 point scale" [20]. The scale is therefore central, not merely departmental — though the central pack dates from 2020, benchmarks are still set per campaign, and the seven descriptor labels themselves remain published only departmentally. What is still open: no central publication of the 4-point strengths descriptors was located; the detail is left to the uk-frameworks deep-dive.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
This is the Tier-2 deep-dive for the customer-service and stakeholder-management competency family — the family that the parent document `competencies-foundation` maps to the Civil Service behaviour **Managing a Quality Service**, to parts of the EPSO general competencies, to NHS patient-focus values, and to the "customer-facing skills" label common in graduate recruitment. Where the parent stays broad, this document goes to the level of published level descriptors, a 38-question bank with per-question provenance, and answer guidance tied to those descriptors.

The central documented finding is that this competency is **not** primarily about being nice to an angry customer. The UK Civil Service defines Managing a Quality Service as "Deliver service objectives with professional excellence, expertise and efficiency, taking account of diverse customer needs" [1], and its published level descriptors escalate from handling individual customers at AA/AO and EO grades to designing, reviewing and benchmarking whole services at HEO/SEO and Grade 7/6 [1]. NHS values-based recruitment approaches the same territory from the patient side, and published NHS trust question banks ask not just about caring for individuals but about seeking dialogue with service users to improve the services they receive [7]. In EPSO's framework the territory is distributed rather than named: service and stakeholder behaviour appears inside the anchors for Communication, Working together and Information management rather than as a standalone competency [3].

Document-level confidence is **Medium**. The UK level descriptors, the EPSO anchors, the NHS values-based recruitment method and the two published scoring scales are official or institutional sources and are quoted directly. However, the private-sector and graduate-scheme portion of the question corpus rests on careers-site question lists, which evidence *what is published as typical* rather than what any named employer asks; and the "one competency, four sub-themes" organisation of the question bank is this knowledge base's own synthesis. Under the confidence rubric, that mix is Medium.
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
- The Civil Service defines the behaviour as delivering service objectives "with professional excellence, expertise and efficiency, taking account of diverse customer needs" — efficiency and cost-effectiveness sit inside the definition, not outside it. *(documented)* [1]
- The published level descriptors shift the unit of analysis with grade: at EO the candidate must "deliver good customer service which balances quality and cost effectiveness"; at HEO/SEO they must "establish ways to find and respond to feedback from customers"; at Grade 7/6 they must "establish how the business area compares to industry best practice". *(documented)* [1]
- Consequently, a story that resolves one complaint beautifully and stops there under-evidences the behaviour at every level above EO. Candidates should carry the answer through to what changed in the service. *(guidance)*
- EPSO has no standalone customer or service competency; the behaviour is evidenced through anchors such as tailoring the message "to respond to the needs of the person or persons with whom they are communicating" and explaining needs "clearly to colleagues, service providers and stakeholders". *(documented)* [3]
- NHS values-based interviewing is a *structured* interview with predetermined questions and predetermined scoring criteria, in which the probing questions — not the headline question — are designed to reveal the candidate's values. *(documented)* [6]
- Two published scoring scales are available to calibrate against: a 1–7 Success Profiles scale where 4 is "adequate positive evidence" and candidates must normally score 4 or more in all behaviours [8], and a 1–5 competency evaluation scheme in which 3 means positive and negative evidence in similar amounts [15]. *(documented)*
- Interviewers should ask each applicant the same questions where possible, use more than one interviewer, and base decisions on the job description and person specification — ACAS guidance that also reduces discrimination risk. *(documented)* [13]
- "Difficult customer" questions are the most published sub-theme in private-sector lists, but the highest-scoring answers in framework terms are usually the service-improvement ones. Prepare both. *(guidance)*
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
This document answers, for candidates: *"When an employer asks about customers, clients, service users or stakeholders, what is actually being scored, and what does an answer that reaches the top of the scale contain?"* And for interviewers and HR: *"How do the published UK, EU and NHS frameworks define this territory, what questions are already published for it, and what indicators can I defensibly score against?"*

It is a child of `competencies-foundation` and assumes that document's material on what competency interviewing is, why structured interviewing is used, and how the eight competency families map across frameworks. That material is not restated here.
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
**Covered:** the Civil Service **Managing a Quality Service** behaviour at published level detail, plus the stakeholder-facing elements of **Working Together** and **Communicating and Influencing** [1]; where service and stakeholder behaviour lives in the EPSO Competency Framework anchors [3]; NHS values-based recruitment as it bears on patient and service-user focus, evidenced from the HEE framework [6] and a published NHS trust question bank [7]; a 38-question bank spanning difficult customers, competing stakeholders, service improvement and follow-up probes; answer guidance and a worked skeleton tied to the cited descriptors; published scoring scales; and the UK/EU legal and fairness points that specifically bite on this competency.

**Not covered:** general competency-interview theory and the family taxonomy (see `competencies-foundation`); STAR and its variants in depth (see `techniques-foundation`); the full Success Profiles or EPSO framework guides (see the UK and EU framework documents); interview formats (see `formats-foundation`); technical customer-service knowledge such as CRM tooling, service-level-agreement drafting or complaint-handling regulation in a specific regulated sector; and sales competencies, which overlap but are assessed against different criteria.
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
- **Managing a Quality Service** — the Civil Service Success Profiles behaviour defined as "Deliver service objectives with professional excellence, expertise and efficiency, taking account of diverse customer needs" [1].
- **Customer** — in the Civil Service descriptors the term is used broadly, internally as well as externally, appearing alongside "colleagues, stakeholders and delivery partners" in the same level descriptors [1]. In this document, "customer" means the recipient of the service, whether an external paying customer, a citizen, a patient or an internal business unit.
- **Stakeholder** — a party with an interest in, or influence over, the service who is not necessarily its recipient. The Civil Service descriptors distinguish stakeholders from customers, requiring candidates at HEO/SEO to "work with stakeholders to set priorities, objectives and timescales" while separately meeting "the customers' needs" [1].
- **Values-based interview (VBI)** — per the HEE Values Based Recruitment Framework, "a type of structured interview which is particularly focused on understanding a candidate's values", in which it is "the probing questions, designed as part of a VBI, that are used to provide insight into a candidate's values" [6].
- **Anchor** — in the EPSO Competency Framework, an observable element of a general competency; the framework "defines the 8 general competencies that EU officials are expected to demonstrate throughout their career", each "composed of various observable elements, called anchors" [3].
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## UK Civil Service: Managing a Quality Service

Managing a Quality Service is one of the nine Civil Service Behaviours within Success Profiles [1] [2]. Its definition is: "Deliver service objectives with professional excellence, expertise and efficiency, taking account of diverse customer needs" [1]. Behaviours are assessed across multiple stages of recruitment — application forms, tests, interviews, assessment centres and job-related simulations — and recruiting managers select the behaviours relevant to a specific role rather than requiring all nine [1].

Two other behaviours carry the stakeholder load. **Working Together** is defined as "Form effective partnerships and relationships with people both internally and externally, from a range of diverse backgrounds, sharing information, resources and support" [1]. **Communicating and Influencing** is defined as "Communicate purpose and direction with clarity, integrity and enthusiasm. Respect the needs, responses and opinions of others" [1]. A candidate asked about a difficult stakeholder in a Civil Service interview is therefore usually being scored against one of these three named behaviours, and the advert states which.

## EU institutions: where service and stakeholder behaviour sits in EPSO's framework

The EPSO Competency Framework in use since 2023 defines eight general competencies — critical thinking, analysing and creative problem-solving; decision-making and getting results; information management; self-management; working together; learning as a skill; communication; and intrapreneurship — each composed of observable anchors [3]. **None of the eight is a customer-service or stakeholder-management competency by name.**

The behaviour is instead distributed across anchors [3]:

- Communication: "Tailors the message to respond to the needs of the person or persons with whom they are communicating."
- Communication: "Takes into account the point of view of others."
- Communication: "Communicates in ways that generate buy-in and create a win-win situation for the parties involved."
- Working together: "Discusses differences of opinion openly with the individuals concerned, understands other points of view, and strives for compromise in situations of conflict."
- Working together: "Offers assistance and help to others."
- Information management: "Indicates and explains technological needs clearly to colleagues, service providers and stakeholders."
- Intrapreneurship: "Acts as a role model and an ambassador for the department."

In an EPSO competency-based interview, candidates are interviewed "for about 10 minutes per competency", with questions structured to let assessors evaluate the selected general competencies for that competition and grade [4].

## NHS: patient focus through values-based recruitment

NHS recruitment in England sits within the NHS Constitution values, which include "working together for patients", "commitment to quality of care", "compassion" and "improving lives" [5]. The HEE Values Based Recruitment Framework expects a structured interview including an assessment of values, and defines structured interviews as ones which "ask a standardised set of predetermined questions so that each candidate receives the same, or very similar, questions", which "are scored against predetermined scoring criteria which are the same for all candidates", and which "interview a candidate on an individual 1:1 basis" [6].

Individual trusts publish their own question banks against their core values. Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust publishes a bank against values including "Caring for Everyone", "Respect for all", "Embracing Teamwork" and "Committed to Improvement", instructing recruiters to ask "a minimum of two questions from the bank of values based questions below, during interviews" [7]. Notably, its "Committed to Improvement" section asks candidates to describe seeking "a dialogue with a service user and their family to improve the services they receive" [7] — the NHS analogue of the Civil Service's HEO/SEO feedback-mechanism requirement.

## Cross-framework mapping

> **Labelling note (synthesis):** the framework cells below are documented [1] [3] [5] [7] [16]; the decision to place them in one row is this knowledge base's reasoned synthesis.

| Sub-theme | Civil Service [1] | EPSO anchors [3] | NHS values and trust banks [5] [7] | Graduate-scheme label [16] |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Understanding customer needs | Managing a Quality Service (all levels) | Communication — "tailors the message to respond to the needs of the person" | "Working together for patients"; "put the needs of others before your own" | Customer-facing skills |
| Handling a difficult customer | Managing a Quality Service (AA/AO, EO) | Working together — "strives for compromise in situations of conflict" | "gone the extra mile in showing that you are caring" | Customer service |
| Managing competing stakeholders | Working Together; Communicating and Influencing | Communication — "generate buy-in and create a win-win situation" | "build successful working relationships with others" | Influencing; stakeholder management |
| Improving the service | Managing a Quality Service (HEO/SEO, G7/6) | Intrapreneurship — "demonstrates initiative, removes barriers and thinks ahead" | "Committed to Improvement"; "improving lives" | Continuous improvement |
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## The published level descriptors (Civil Service, Managing a Quality Service)

These are the most granular published criteria available for this competency in either market, and they are the backbone of this document. Quoted from GOV.UK [1].

**AA/AO grade.** The candidate is expected to: "gain a clear understanding of customers' needs and expectations"; "plan, organise and manage your own time to deliver a high quality service"; "act to prevent problems by identifying issues, reporting them and providing solutions"; "keep customers and all colleagues up to date with progress"; and "show customers where to access relevant information and support" [1].

**EO grade.** The candidate is expected to: "work with customers to understand their needs and expectations"; "create clear plans and set priorities which meet the needs of both customer and business"; "clearly explain to customers what can be done"; "keep colleagues and stakeholders fully informed of plans, possibilities and progress"; "identify common problems that affect service, report them and find possible solutions"; and "deliver good customer service which balances quality and cost effectiveness" [1].

**HEO/SEO grade.** The candidate is expected to: "develop, implement, maintain and review systems and services to ensure delivery of professional excellence"; "work with stakeholders to set priorities, objectives and timescales"; "successfully deliver high quality outcomes that meet the customers' needs and gives value for money"; "identify risks and resolve issues efficiently"; "involve a diverse range of colleagues, stakeholders and delivery partners in developing suggestions"; and "establish ways to find and respond to feedback from customers about the services provided" [1].

**Grade 7/6.** The candidate is expected to: "demonstrate positive customer service by understanding the complexity and diversity of customer needs"; "deliver a high quality, efficient and cost effective service by considering a broad range of methods"; "ensure full consideration of new technologies, accessibility and costings"; "make clear, practical and manageable plans for service delivery"; "ensure adherence to legal, regulatory and security requirements in service delivery"; "proactively manage risks and identify solutions"; "establish how the business area compares to industry best practice"; and "create regular opportunities for colleagues, stakeholders, delivery partners and customers to improve service" [1].

At Deputy Director level the emphasis moves to clarifying customer requirements, using insight for high-quality delivery, collaborating with partners and breaking complex aims into plans; at Director and Director General level it moves to ensuring delivery across government, maintaining an evolving understanding of customers, negotiating service levels and ensuring stakeholder clarity on customer impacts [1].

Three structural observations follow directly from the quoted text. First, **cost and efficiency are inside the competency** from EO upwards ("balances quality and cost effectiveness"; "value for money"; "cost effective service") [1]. Second, **feedback machinery becomes an explicit requirement at HEO/SEO** ("establish ways to find and respond to feedback") [1]. Third, **accessibility and diversity of need are explicit at Grade 7/6** ("new technologies, accessibility and costings"; "complexity and diversity of customer needs") [1], which links this competency directly to the reasonable-adjustment duty discussed under Legal & Fairness below [10].

## What the published question corpus actually contains

The published question sets divide unevenly. Private-sector and careers-service lists cluster heavily on the individual difficult-customer encounter: Indeed's UK customer-service article asks candidates to "Describe a time you dealt with a difficult customer and how you resolved the issue" and to "Describe a time you helped a customer who had spoken to multiple agents without getting a solution" [18]; My World of Work lists "Describe a time you had to handle a customer's difficult request" [17]; targetjobs lists "When have you previously delivered excellent customer service?" [16].

Institutional question banks reach further up the descriptor ladder. The Wirral trust bank includes questions on making "an improvement in your work area, role or service", on seeking "a dialogue with a service user and their family to improve the services they receive", and on what happened "when you've been given feedback on services by a service user, around what they'd like to see change" [7]. That asymmetry — careers sites publish the encounter, institutions publish the improvement loop — is the single most useful fact in this document for a candidate deciding what to prepare.

## Why structured questioning is used here

CIPD guidance states that structured interviews are better at predicting job performance than unstructured interviews, that situational and past-behavioural questions are the best kinds of interview questions, and that consistent questions with objective scoring criteria make responses comparable and reduce the influence of personal bias [14]. The academic re-analysis by Sackett, Zhang, Berry and Lievens found structured interviews to be the top-ranked selection procedure for predicting job performance after correcting for systematic overcorrection for restriction of range [20].
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
Thirty-eight questions across four sub-themes. *(sourced …)* questions are quoted from the named published source and carry a citation; *(composed)* questions were written for this knowledge base in the style of the cited frameworks and are **not** attributed to any employer.

## Difficult customers and complaints

1. Describe a time you had to handle a customer's difficult request. *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [17]
2. Describe a time you dealt with a difficult customer and how you resolved the issue. *(sourced: Indeed UK, customer service interview questions)* [18]
3. Describe a time you helped a customer who had spoken to multiple agents without getting a solution. *(sourced: Indeed UK, customer service interview questions)* [18]
4. How would you handle a customer who was upset about a known problem with your product or service? *(sourced: Indeed UK, customer service interview questions)* [18]
5. What does customer service mean to you? *(sourced: Indeed UK, customer service interview questions)* [18]
6. When have you previously delivered excellent customer service? *(sourced: targetjobs)* [16]
7. Can you tell me/us about a time when you have gone the extra mile in showing that you are caring towards others? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
8. Describe a time when you have put the needs of others before your own and how you did this. *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
9. What would be your approach when you come into contact with a patient, visitor or staff member you have never met before? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
10. Tell me about a time a customer was angry and, on investigation, was entirely right — your organisation had got it wrong. What did you do? *(composed)*
11. Describe a situation where you had to tell a customer that what they wanted was not possible. How did you handle the conversation? *(composed)*
12. Tell me about a time you had to deliver bad news to a customer about a delay or a failure. How did you keep their trust? *(composed)*

## Competing stakeholders and conflicting priorities

1. Tell me about a time when you have had to balance or juggle a number of (conflicting) priorities. *(sourced: University of Oxford Careers Service, Example interview questions)* [15]
2. Tell me about a time when you have been faced with resistance or negativity — how did you respond? *(sourced: University of Oxford Careers Service, Example interview questions)* [15]
3. Describe a situation in which you were able to use persuasion to successfully convince colleagues of a different perspective whilst taking into account their points of view. What did you do and what was the outcome? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
4. Tell me about a time that you feel demonstrates your ability to build successful working relationships with others. What was the positive impact of this? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
5. Tell me about a time when you feel that your communication skills made a difference to the situation. *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
6. Describe a time two stakeholders wanted incompatible things from the same piece of work. How did you decide what to do? *(composed)*
7. Tell me about a time you had to manage the expectations of a stakeholder more senior than you. *(composed)*
8. Give me an example of a time you had to keep stakeholders informed about work that was going badly. *(composed)*
9. Describe a situation where you had to rebuild a working relationship with a stakeholder that had broken down. *(composed)*
10. Tell me about a time you had to agree service levels or timescales with a partner organisation. How did you reach agreement? *(composed)*

## Service improvement and quality

1. Tell me/us about a time when you have made an improvement in your work area, role or service and what you did to achieve this? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
2. Please describe a situation where you have actively sought a dialogue with a service user and their family to improve the services they receive. How did you go about doing this; what was involved? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
3. What has happened in the past when you've been given feedback on services by a service user, around what they'd like to see change? How did you progress this? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
4. If you identified something that needed to improve, what would be your approach to take this forward? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
5. How would you work with your team to build continuous improvement into services? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
6. How do you ensure that patients are always at the forefront of what you do? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
7. Give me an example of a time you set up a way of collecting feedback from the people who use your service, and what you changed as a result. *(composed, in the style of the HEO/SEO descriptor on establishing ways to find and respond to customer feedback)* [1]
8. Tell me about a time you had to deliver a service that balanced quality against cost. What trade-off did you make and how did you justify it? *(composed, in the style of the EO descriptor on balancing quality and cost effectiveness)* [1]
9. Describe a time you compared how your team or service performed against others, inside or outside your organisation. What did you do with what you found? *(composed, in the style of the Grade 7/6 descriptor on comparison to industry best practice)* [1]
10. Tell me about a time you changed a service to make it accessible to people whose needs were not being met. *(composed, in the style of the Grade 7/6 descriptor on technologies, accessibility and costings)* [1]
11. Describe a recurring problem that affected your customers. How did you identify the pattern, and what did you do about the cause rather than the symptom? *(composed)*

## Follow-up probes

Assessors rarely stop at the headline question. In values-based interviewing it is explicitly "the probing questions … that are used to provide insight into a candidate's values" [6]. Prepare for these.

1. What was the outcome? How did this make you feel? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
2. Considering the outcome of the above, if it was successful, why do you think it was successful? If it wasn't successful, why do you think this was the case and what would you do differently? *(sourced: Wirral University Teaching Hospital NHS Foundation Trust)* [7]
3. How did you manage conflicting ideas? *(sourced: University of Oxford Careers Service, Example interview questions)* [15]
4. What did you learn from the experience? What would you do differently in future? *(sourced: University of Oxford Careers Service, Example interview questions)* [15]
5. What was the solution — who suggested it? If others were involved, how did you persuade them to your point of view? *(sourced: University of Oxford Careers Service, Example interview questions)* [15]
6. How did you know the customer was satisfied at the end? What did they actually say or do? *(composed)*
7. What did that cost, in time or money, and was it worth it? *(composed)*
8. Did anything change in the service afterwards, or did the problem stay solved only for that one customer? *(composed)*
9. Who else needed to be involved, and how did you bring them in? *(composed)*
10. If the same situation arose tomorrow with twice the volume, what would you do differently? *(composed)*
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.**

## Choosing which example to use

The descriptors above [1] give a mechanical selection test. Read the grade or seniority of the role, then pick an example whose *unit of analysis* matches:

- **Entry level (AA/AO and EO analogues; retail, contact centre, first graduate role):** an individual customer encounter is appropriate, but the answer must still show understanding of the customer's need, a plan, clear explanation of what could be done, and keeping people updated [1].
- **Mid level (HEO/SEO analogues; team lead, consultant, analyst):** an individual encounter is *not enough*. The example should involve stakeholders, priorities and timescales, and ideally a feedback mechanism [1].
- **Senior level (Grade 7/6 and above):** the example should be about a service, not a transaction — plans, risks, legal and regulatory adherence, benchmarking, and opportunities for others to improve the service [1].

For EU-institution interviews, remember there is no service competency to name; select the example that best evidences the *Communication* or *Working together* anchors, and use language that maps onto them — tailoring the message to the person's needs, taking others' points of view into account, striving for compromise [3]. With roughly ten minutes per competency [4], one well-developed example per competency is the right amount of material.

## Worked STAR skeleton

Question: *"Give me an example of a time you set up a way of collecting feedback from the people who use your service, and what you changed as a result."* (composed question 7 under Service improvement).

- **Situation (brief).** "I ran the enquiries inbox for a 200-person department. We were closing tickets quickly, but the same three questions kept coming back every month."
- **Task (brief).** "I wanted to know whether we were actually solving anything, so I proposed a short post-resolution survey and a monthly review of ticket categories."
- **Action (the bulk of the answer).** "I agreed the two survey questions with our team lead so it took under 20 seconds to answer. I categorised six months of past tickets and found 41% clustered into three topics. I took that to the service owner and the two teams whose processes generated the queries — I framed it in their terms, as avoidable work landing on them, not as a complaint about them. We agreed I would draft self-service guidance for the top three topics and they would review it."
- **Result.** "Survey response rate was 34%; satisfaction with resolutions rose from 3.4 to 4.1 out of 5 over the following quarter, and tickets in those three categories fell by roughly a third, which freed about half a day a week across the team. We kept the monthly category review as a standing item."
- **Reflection (optional, one sentence).** "What I would do differently is agree the measure with the stakeholders *before* building the survey — I had to redo the categories once."

Why this skeleton scores: it evidences "establish ways to find and respond to feedback from customers about the services provided", "involve a diverse range of colleagues, stakeholders and delivery partners in developing suggestions" and "develop, implement, maintain and review systems and services", all HEO/SEO-level descriptor language [1]. It also carries the EPSO Communication anchor about generating buy-in and creating a win-win [3], because the stakeholder framing is explicit in the Action. The STAR structure itself follows the National Careers Service formulation of Situation, Task, Action and Result including what you learned [19].

## Answering the difficult-customer question well

The published difficult-customer questions [17] [18] are easy to answer adequately and hard to answer distinctively. Three moves lift the answer against the cited descriptors:

- **Separate the emotion from the problem, out loud.** Say what you did to understand the need before you say what you fixed — the descriptors at every level begin with understanding customers' needs and expectations [1].
- **Say what you could and could not do.** The EO descriptor asks candidates to "clearly explain to customers what can be done" [1]; a candidate who describes setting an honest boundary usually gives more scoreable evidence than one who implies the customer got everything they asked for.
- **Finish above the transaction.** Add one sentence on whether the cause was systemic and what you did about it. This is the bridge from an AA/AO-level story to an HEO/SEO-level one [1].

## Interviewer-side guidance

Decide before the interview which behaviour a question is scoring — a difficult-stakeholder question can legitimately sit under Working Together, Communicating and Influencing or Managing a Quality Service [1], and the criteria differ. Agree the probes in advance too, since in values-based practice the probes carry the diagnostic weight [6].
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## How answers are scored

Two published scales are available.

The Crown Office and Procurator Fiscal Service publishes the Success Profiles scoring approach it uses: applications and interviews are "scored using the 7 point scale", running from "Not Demonstrated" (1) to "Outstanding Demonstration" (7), where scores 1–3 indicate negative or limited evidence and 4 represents "adequate positive evidence" [8]. On benchmarks, its guidance states that "candidates must demonstrate the minimum essential criteria for all roles, this scoring 4 or more in all behaviours", while noting that a candidate can score a single 3 where scores for all other behaviours are high [8]. Assessors look for positive evidence of behaviours demonstrated through actions and activities, and the board uses probing questions to understand the situation, the actions taken and the outcomes [8].

The University of Oxford Careers Service reproduces a five-point competency evaluation scheme, attributed to Civil Service Resourcing best-practice recruitment guidance, in which 5 is "demonstrates positive evidence of a high level of competence with no substantial negative evidence", 4 is "demonstrates positive evidence of competence with little negative evidence", 3 is "demonstrates similar amounts of positive evidence and negative evidence of competence", 2 is "demonstrates some positive evidence of competence, but with significant negative evidence", and 1 is "demonstrates little or no positive evidence of competence, along with considerable negative evidence" [15].

Both scales are **evidence-balance** scales, not impression scales. The practical consequence for this competency is that a story containing a strong resolution *and* an unaddressed admission — for example, that the same failure kept recurring — can score at the midpoint rather than high, because negative evidence is weighed, not ignored.

## What assessors are listening for

- **Behaviour matched to anchors, not opinions.** The EPSO framework decomposes each competency into observable anchors [3]; assessors listen for described behaviour matching those anchors.
- **The probes, not the headline.** In values-based interviewing, the probing questions are the instrument designed to provide insight into the candidate's values [6].
- **Consistency across candidates.** HEE requires structured interviews scored "against predetermined scoring criteria which are the same for all candidates" [6]; ACAS advises preparing questions in advance, asking each applicant the same questions where possible, using more than one interviewer to reduce the risk of discriminating, and checking that decisions are based on how each applicant met each point in the job description and person specification [13].
- **Selected behaviours only.** Recruiting managers choose which behaviours a role is assessed against rather than requiring all nine, so the advert tells candidates what will be scored [1].
- **Time budget.** In EPSO competency-based interviews, candidates are interviewed for about ten minutes per competency [4], which bounds how much detail an answer can carry.
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.**

- **Resolving the complaint but showing no service-improvement thinking.** The most common failure on this competency. It under-evidences everything above EO level: the HEO/SEO descriptor requires establishing ways to find and respond to feedback and reviewing systems and services [1], and a story that ends at "the customer left happy" simply contains no evidence for it. On an evidence-balance scale [8] [15] this is not so much a low score as a capped one.
- **Ignoring cost entirely.** Candidates often describe heroic effort for one customer. But "balances quality and cost effectiveness" is in the EO descriptor, and "value for money" and "cost effective service" appear at HEO/SEO and Grade 7/6 [1]. An answer where the candidate spent unbounded time on one case, with no acknowledgement of the trade-off, invites a negative-evidence mark.
- **Treating "stakeholder" as "person who was annoying".** The framework language is about partnerships and relationships, and about setting priorities, objectives and timescales with stakeholders [1]. An answer that frames the stakeholder as an obstacle to be managed around, rather than a party to be worked with, runs against the EPSO Working together anchor on discussing differences openly and striving for compromise [3].
- **The rescued-by-someone-else story.** Assessors score your actions; the COPFS guidance is explicit that they look for evidence of behaviours demonstrated through actions and activities [8]. Escalating is legitimate, but the answer needs your judgement in it.
- **Hypothetical answers to past-behaviour questions.** Several published questions in the bank above are deliberately future-framed ("How would you work with your team to…" [7]) and others are past-framed. Answer in the tense asked; an "I would…" answer to a "tell me about a time…" question provides no behavioural evidence.
- **Forgetting diversity of need.** Grade 7/6 requires "understanding the complexity and diversity of customer needs" and consideration of accessibility [1]; NHS trust banks ask directly about respect for cultural and individual differences [7]. An answer that treats all customers as identical misses available marks.
- **For interviewers: scoring a service question against a communication rubric.** If the advert names the behaviour, score that behaviour [1]; drifting criteria reintroduce the comparability problems that structured interviewing exists to solve [14].
- **For interviewers: improvising probes.** ACAS advises the same questions where possible for consistency and fairness [13], and HEE requires the same predetermined criteria for all candidates [6]. Probes should be planned, not invented per candidate.
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
**Coaching layer.**

1. **The ladder drill (45 minutes).** Take one real customer story. Write it three times: once as an AA/AO-level answer, once at HEO/SEO, once at Grade 7/6, using the quoted descriptors [1] as your checklist. Most people discover their story only reaches the first rung — that tells you what evidence you need to go and find.
2. **Self-score on a published scale (30 minutes).** Record a three-minute answer to Difficult customers question 2 [18]. Play it back and score yourself 1–5 on the evaluation scheme reproduced by Oxford [15], writing down each piece of positive evidence *and* each piece of negative evidence. If you cannot list at least three positive items, the answer is not yet a 4.
3. **The probe gauntlet (20 minutes, pairs).** Have a partner ask you one Service improvement question, then all five sourced follow-up probes in sequence [7] [15]. Answers that survive the probes are the ones worth taking to interview.
4. **Anchor translation for EPSO (20 minutes).** Take your best stakeholder story and rewrite the Action section so it evidences at least three named EPSO anchors [3] — for example tailoring the message to the person's needs, taking others' points of view into account, and striving for compromise. Say the anchor language aloud; it should sound natural, not recited.
5. **Cost sentence (10 minutes).** For each of your prepared service examples, write one sentence stating what the solution cost in time, money or opportunity, and why it was proportionate. Rehearse until it is a natural part of the Result.
6. **Interviewer drill (45 minutes, pairs).** Choose three questions from the bank, decide in advance which behaviour each scores [1], write the positive and negative indicators you will look for, agree three probes, then run a mock and compare scores. Disagreement between the two of you usually means the indicators were too vague — rewrite them.
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited. Signposting only — not legal advice.**

- **Accessibility is part of the competency, not just of the interview.** The Grade 7/6 descriptor requires "full consideration of new technologies, accessibility and costings" in service delivery [1]. In the UK this connects to the Equality Act 2010, Part 5 of which covers work [9] and section 20 of which sets out the duty to make reasonable adjustments [10]. Candidates describing service design should expect accessibility to be a legitimate probe; interviewers should not treat it as an optional extra.
- **Adjustments in the interview itself.** ACAS states that by law employers must ask anyone coming to an interview to tell them if they need reasonable adjustments so they can attend [13]. This matters disproportionately for this competency because customer-service assessment often includes role-plays and simulations, which can disadvantage some disabled candidates without adjustment.
- **Consistency as a fairness control.** ACAS advises preparing questions in advance, asking each applicant the same questions where possible, and using more than one interviewer to reduce the risk of discriminating [13]; CIPD notes that consistent questions and objective scoring criteria reduce the influence of personal bias [14]. HEE builds the same control into values-based recruitment through predetermined questions and criteria [6].
- **EU equal treatment.** Council Directive 2000/78/EC establishes a general framework for equal treatment in employment and occupation, expressly covering conditions for access to employment including selection criteria and recruitment conditions, and imposes reasonable-accommodation obligations in respect of disabled persons [11].
- **Candidate data.** Interview notes and scores about identifiable candidates are personal data; in the EU their processing falls under Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR) [12]. Where a customer-service assessment involves recording role-plays, the recording is candidate personal data and should be handled accordingly.
- **A competency-specific caution.** Questions about "cultural fit with our customers", or about how customers might react to a candidate, risk importing third-party prejudice into a selection decision. ACAS guidance that decisions must rest on how each applicant met the job description and person specification [13] is the safer footing.
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
- **UK-weighted evidence.** The strongest published criteria for this competency are British. EPSO has no named customer or stakeholder competency [3], so the EU material in this document is necessarily inferential in its *organisation*, even though every anchor quoted is verbatim.
- **A single trust's question bank.** The NHS question corpus here is drawn from one trust's published bank [7], chosen because it is publicly available and unusually complete. Other trusts phrase questions against their own values; treat these as representative of published NHS practice, not as a national standard. A second trust bank (NHS Blood and Transplant) was identified in search results but its host did not resolve at the time of verification and it is therefore not cited.
- **Careers-site questions evidence what is published, not what is asked.** The targetjobs, My World of Work and Indeed questions [16] [17] [18] show what is published as typical; none is attributable to a named employer's actual process.
- **The 1–7 scale is central; the benchmarks and the 1–5 scheme are not.** Cabinet Office guidance states that "Experience, technical skills and behaviours should be scored using the 7 point scale. Strengths should be scored using the 4 point scale" [21], so the 7-point scale is Civil Service-wide; COPFS [8] is a departmental publication of it and remains the only located source for the seven descriptor labels. Note that the central pack dates from 2020, with no refreshed central scoring publication found after the January 2025 behaviour-guide refresh. Benchmarks are still set per campaign, and the 1–5 scheme is a separate scale reproduced by a university careers service from Civil Service Resourcing guidance [15]. Use these to understand the *logic* of evidence-balance scoring, not to predict a specific employer's mark scheme.
- **Level descriptors are periodically revised.** The Success Profiles behaviours are maintained on GOV.UK [2] and the descriptors quoted here were verified on 2026-07-20; the HEE Values Based Recruitment Framework dates from March 2016 [6] and HEE has since been absorbed into NHS England, so its status should be re-checked before high-stakes use. Review is due 2027-07-20.
- **Private-sector depth is thin.** Consulting, financial-services and technology employers demonstrably ask client and stakeholder questions, but the specific published sources located for this document were general careers sites rather than named corporate frameworks. See Open Questions.
title: Customer Service & Stakeholder Management: Question Bank & Answer Guidance

text:
- **Named private-sector competency dictionaries.** No publicly available, named consultancy or bank competency dictionary covering client service was located during verification; two large-firm careers pages were checked and carried no example questions. Obtaining one would raise the private-sector portion of this document from Medium towards High confidence.
- **Current status of the HEE Values Based Recruitment Framework under NHS England.** Whether the March 2016 framework [6] remains the operative national guidance after HEE's merger into NHS England could not be confirmed from a primary source; a current NHS England statement would resolve it.
- **A second NHS trust question bank.** The NHS Blood and Transplant values-based question document appeared in search results but its host did not resolve during verification. Corroborating the Wirral bank [7] against a second published trust bank would strengthen the NHS section.
- **EPSO situational interview treatment of service scenarios.** Whether EPSO's situational competency-based interview assignments include service or stakeholder scenarios, and how they are scored, was not verified here and belongs to the EU frameworks deep-dive.
- **Role-play and simulation criteria.** Customer-service selection frequently uses role-plays, and the Civil Service uses job-related simulations to assess behaviours [1]; published marking criteria for customer-service role-plays were not located and would be a valuable addition.
